The third port is being introduced, and the camera view is adjusted to a higher perspective.
The surgeon inserts two 5mm instruments into the chest cavity, revealing the pericardium and diaphragm. Adhesions are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the use of a hook ligature device, which transitions from a ligature to a hook, facilitating the thymectomy procedure with minimal instrumentation.

The surgeon uses the Ligasure sealing technology to divide small vessels, including the thymic vein and the rhema vein. The right lung is being carefully separated from the thymus, with the phrenic nerve clearly visible on the superior vena cava (SVC). The Ligasure device is used with precision, minimizing heat dissipation to prevent damage to surrounding structures.
The surgeon points out the phrenic nerve, which is visible adjacent to the surgical field.
The surgeon clears fatty tissue from the diaphragmatic recess after separating the lung from the thymus, ensuring complete removal of thymic and fatty tissue.

The surgeon removes tissue using a sub-xiphoid port, which accommodates a twelve millimeter trocar and a thirty-degree camera to visualize the opposite phrenic nerve. This approach facilitates the removal of thymic tumors of various sizes without extensively opening intercostal spaces.

The surgeon uses a hook instrument to clean the recess between the pericardium and the diaphragm, then proceeds to open the left pleura. CO₂ is insufflated at eight to ten millimeters of mercury to enhance visualization.

The surgeon enters the left pleura and re-expands the right lung using a double lumen tube, allowing ventilation of both lungs. The CO₂ insufflation pushes both lungs downward to maintain exposure throughout the procedure.
The surgeon enters the pericardium early in the procedure, preparing to excise a large disc of pericardium along with the thymic mass, utilizing CO₂ insufflation to facilitate the thymectomy.

The surgeon strips the thymus and surrounding thymic fat from below upwards, carefully managing the pericardiofrenic vessels on both sides using a ligature.

The surgeon carefully follows the lateral border of the thymus, approaching the area near the phrenic nerve. The ligature is used to open the area, ensuring no thymic tissue is left behind, while avoiding the use of a hook due to the proximity to the phrenic nerve.

The surgeon dissects on the right side, revealing the superior vena cava (SVC) and opening the pleural reflection beyond the reamer vein. The innominate vein is visible tracking medially as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon uses a hook to dissect medial to the rhema vein, efficiently separating tissue over the thymic mass. The dissection is sped up for time efficiency.

The surgeon uses a ligasure device to address small vessels descending from the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) and right internal mammary artery (RIMA), then proceeds to strip the thymus from the posterior surface of the sternum, ensuring the opposite pleura is opened widely.

The surgeon opens the pleura widely on the opposite side, revealing the lemur. The pericardium is opened, leaving a large disc intact, providing a clear view of the surgical field. The thymic mass is visible without any invasion through the pericardium, and there are no adhesions present.
The surgeon opens the pericardium widely, ensuring there is no invasion of the thymic mass. The pericardium is removed, revealing two intact lungs, and there is no risk of heart herniation.

The surgeon opens the surgical field superiorly, focusing on the area around the superior horns of the thymus. The opposite phrenic nerve is visible, highlighting the importance of careful visualization during this part of the thymectomy.

The surgeon carefully dissects the fatty tissue and thymus from the area beyond the reamer vein, which is clearly visible. Anticipating vessels from the reamer and lemur veins and arteries, the surgeon proceeds cautiously, expecting to encounter a vessel laterally.

The surgeon dissects over the innominate vein, anticipating the presence of a thymic vein extending into the thymus. A ligature device is used to cauterize any encountered vessels, avoiding the use of clips due to their potential instability during further dissection.
The surgeon encounters a large, adherent thymic mass and navigates around sticky areas on the innominate vein. They proceed to dissect over the top, aiming to locate the lemur vein and artery.

The surgeon performs a 12mm vertical incision just below the xiphisternum for a sub-xiphoid port, inserting a finger followed by a Roberts retractor, directing the instrument upwards above the diaphragm. The camera is used to visualize the left phrenic nerve.

The surgeon uses a sub-xiphoid camera and two instruments from the right side to attempt to pull the thymoma across, encountering adhesions to the lung on both the right and left sides. The surgeon returns to dissect further over the innominate vein.
The surgeon examines the surgical field, focusing on the lemur and reamer vein and artery. A vessel of considerable size is identified, suitable for energy-based dissection.

The surgeon tracks up into the right superior horn of the thymus, aiming to dissect up to the thyro-thymic ligament. The dissection avoids cutting across fatty tissue, revealing a tumor extending further superiorly than anticipated.

The surgeon encounters the Rheema vein obstructing the view and uses a ligature to safely divide it, enhancing visibility. This allows for better access to the superior horn, facilitating dissection up to the thyro-thymic ligament.

The surgeon has removed the fatty tissue, revealing ligamentous tissue with visible artery and vein at the correct level. The surgeon releases tissue up into the neck and uses a technique of pressing on the sternal notch to gauge the height of the dissection, occasionally seeing their finger through the surgical field.

The surgeon is dissecting the right superior horn, revealing the innominate vein and artery beneath the tumor. The innominate artery is visible, indicating the correct dissection plane. The surgeon uses a ligature to manage a vein from the SVC and innominate, while firmly pulling down the tissue to ensure no fatty tissue remains above. Tumor presence is noted, and the surgeon prepares to cut across the tissue.

The surgeon pulls the left superior horn southward, achieving a clear view from the right-hand side. The horn is extracted from the neck and pushed into the opposite chest for retraction. The lima vein and artery are ligated, addressing the blood supply from the lima and rima vein and artery.

The surgeon uses a single five-millimeter port on the left side to maintain a clear view of the phrenic nerve while lifting the tumor. The ligasure device is employed with low heat to carefully separate the tumor from the phrenic nerve. A previously divided piece of lung is visible. Once the phrenic nerve is pushed aside, the pericardium is exposed and can be safely divided.

The surgeon visualizes the pulmonary artery and innominate vein, ensuring they are clear of the surgical field. The phrenic nerve is identified, and dissection is performed close to it to remove fatty tissue beneath the left phrenic.
